"Use the same langauge for printers as for screens", he may refer to this <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NeWS> and <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Display_PostScript>

attachments.title: NeWS
attachments.text: NeWS (Network extensible Window System) is a discontinued windowing system developed by Sun Microsystems in the mid-1980s. Originally known as "SunDew", its primary authors were James Gosling and David S. H. Rosenthal. The NeWS interpreter was based on PostScript (as was the later Display PostScript, although the two projects were otherwise unrelated) extending it to allow interaction and multiple "contexts" to support windows. Like PostScript, NeWS could be used as a complete programming language, but unlike PostScript, NeWS could be used to make complete interactive programs with mouse support and a GUI.

attachments.title: Display PostScript
attachments.text:
Display PostScript (or DPS) is a 2D graphics engine system for computers which uses the PostScript (PS) imaging model and language (originally developed for computer printing) to generate on-screen graphics. To the basic PS system, DPS adds a number of features intended to ease working with bitmapped displays and improve performance of some common tasks.
Early versions of PostScript display systems were developed at Adobe Systems. During development of the NeXT computers, NeXT and Adobe collaborated to produce the official DPS system, which was released in 1987. NeXT used DPS throughout its history, while versions from Adobe were popular on Unix workstations for a time during the 1980s and 1990s.

The quality of the write-up is excellent.

I'm skeptical of "Reading a couple hundred books a year is the bare minimum. It’s just the baseline." It's the baseline to get to where Alan Kay is now, for better and for worse. But I suspect you need more than Alan Kay to get where you're going. Too much book learning could be where he went astray. At a couple hundred books per year you'd finish his recommendations in a year and still have time to do things in later years. Or you could read 20 books per year for ten years and actually play with some of the ideas you're reading about.

I don't mean to say that Alan Kay is just reading books without doing anything, year in and year out. Just using contrast for rhetorical purpose. I don't actually know what missteps projects like Dynabook or STEPS may have made. But it's worth keeping those possibilities on your radar to avoid survivor bias in your thinking.

At the risk of going against the flow, I want to put forth the suggestion that maybe Alan Kay isn't quite the messiah he's sometimes worked up to be. He's an intelligent man for sure, but to some degree eccentric, and it's unclear whether his advice is actually good advice. He did contribute to some major advances in the 20th century, but his achievements in recent decades are more minor, so it doesn't appear his formula is working out even for himself anymore.

One requirement of being a leader (including a thought leader) is being a good speaker and salesman. Alan Kay definitely fits that bill, but you have to peel back the presentation layer and look beneath to see if the underlying content is any good. Donald Trump is also a good salesman, but because we (tech people) are not his target audience, most of us here have likely concluded that the content he's selling is of questionable quality. With salesman who are targeting us (tech people) directly, I think it's harder to see the reality of the content. Personally, after casually buying Alan Kay's content for a few years, I've since become skeptical of it.

<@UCGAK10LS>, I want to agree that Alan Kay, like anyone else, deserves both respect and critique.  For example, it's quite fair to consider whether STEPS was a good idea for a project.  What came up it?  What went right?  What went wrong?  Whether it was successful?  By what measure?  And what can we learn from it?

<@UEG9ECNSU> Thanks. I haven’t seen the Sun Ra movie, but I’ll add it to my queue. About your gist: I feel similarly, wondering how my life would have been different if I lived somewhere more important, or conducted my time differently when I was younger. But <@UCGAK10LS> has the right take: don’t celebrate celebrity. If everyone in the world needs to end up in the top 1% of some field, then most people will live unfulfilled lives. Better to pick something you want to do well, and do it well on your own terms. I’ll be happy if the programming system I’m making helps my coworkers enjoy their job more. I don’t need to make an impact that lasts for 10,000 years — that’s an awful goal to choose for oneself.

I respect his interdisciplinary approach and broad vision. I think insights come from unexpected places, in my case I've had a lot of programming insight from works of philosophy such as Quine and Hegel. I can't help but feel a whiff of "hipsterism" in his suggestions though ... they're so obscure, he points a computer scientist not just at Adam Smith but at lesser-known Adam Smith. The more obvious reading suggestions like say Knuth are absent, does he think it's not worth fitting in Knuth to your 200 a year because McLuhan is more relevant to CS, or is he just assuming that suggesting Knuth to a CS person is redundant?

I agree completely about making the kernel of the Web as simple as possible, I'm kind of baffled why there's so little progress in that direction, e.g. I haven't even heard of an academic project to build a small version of the web. NaCl was the closest thing to such an effort that I've heard of (I guess wasm takes up the mantle but most wasm people disclaim the idea of ever deprecating the overgrown web stack).

<@UH84GQ3CZ> will reading Knuth give you any new perspective or insight on how to think about computing and media? From what I see Kay seems deeply interested in different and insightful perspectives rather than deep refinement and study through one perspective.

<@UH84GQ3CZ> will reading Knuth give you any new perspective or insight on how to think about computing and media? From what I see Kay seems deeply interested in different and insightful perspectives rather than deep refinement and study through one perspective.

Also Smalltalk-OO is an attempt to finding a perspective very different from 'data structures + procedures'.
> I thought of the whole as the entire computer and wondered why anyone would want to divide it up into weaker things called data structures and procedures.
So, to put it in meme form: Knuth is tired.

<@U8A5MS6R1> if you are trying to write a new programming tool or language or system, then I think you'll find yourself working with syntax trees and grammars, and if you do that without learning about concepts like LR parsing or attribute grammars then I think you're missing some significant and relevant insights. Certainly I think that Knuth would help you more than McLuhan.

<@UH84GQ3CZ> - Perhaps. I think there is a leap here: "if you are trying to write a new programming tool or language or system, then I think you'll find yourself working with syntax trees and grammars". Yes if you go down this path certainly Knuth etc. are useful. But how do you choose which path you want to explore or what different paths are even available? Are there other paths where you have programmable systems *without* complex parsing? What could these look like? For the broader problem I think we could take a holistic outlook where computation machines, cognitive psychology, HCI all blur together.

<@UH84GQ3CZ> - Perhaps. Some more thoughts.

> Alan spent most of the lunch intellectually destroying me, smacking me around, and pointing out how much more I have to learn
Alan is the 'CS research gadfly', perhaps. Like how Socrates was the social gadfly "to sting people and whip them into a fury, all in the service of truth." - Plato on Socrates in his defense.
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_gadfly>

Regarding goals, this reminded me of the philosophical question "Is there true altruism?"

Compare
> Research is all about progress.. No ego
and
> Alan helped show me that I am professing to be benevolent when I really just wanted to get all the credit for “democratizing programming.”
vs.
> It seemed like Alan was asking me to pick: how benevolent do you actually want to be?

Is there no ego in the last one?

Sorry I think I ranted on this before but I vehemently disagree with making the web kernel smaller. I used to have that opinion and I changed my mind.

I'm assuming by that people mean they just want some low-level VM like thing with pixels and input. So let's consider some of the issues

• Everyone renders their own text using their own font data and their own font renderers.

Result: Internationalization is worse than ever. Trying to type Chinese, Japanese, Korean, fails in 99% of apps.

• Everyone renders text to pixels

Result: No way to write ad blockers since there is no structure to the data. It's just a block of pixels. No way to write translation extensions like Rikaikun. No way for Google Translate to translate a page because it can't scan the document. At best it could only OCR what's on the screen at the moment assuming the page doesn't have some fancy background making OCR too hard. No way to select, copy and paste text. Again there's no standard structure so unless the app itself decides to implement highlighting text with the cursor and letting you copy that text to a clipboard you're out of luck.

• No blocking videos

Videos are currently blockable because they are generally implemented using a `<video>` tag and because the protocols are standardized. In a 'we draw video using our own codec to pixel world' no more blocking video since there is no way for the system to know that you're drawing pixels for text or video, it's all just pixels.

• No support for Accessibility tech

Again, because the common format is HTML it's easy to write something that translates to braille or reads the words aloud. In a "it's a bucket of pixels and all data is opaque" that disappears

• Hyperlinks disappear

If everyone is making up their own display to pixels tech then there are no links

There are many many similar issues. I know browsers have their issues but I disagree the world would be better with a low-level "cross platform assembly + drawing surface"

I also know some of the issues above could be kind of solved by standards and praying that every developer in the world follows those standards and that they all update to the new standards as the standards change.

My impression is that by having the standard be the default method of creation it leads to far more people following the standards since it's the path of least resistance.
